sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Brandon, is a 36-year-old male who presents with neck pain following multiple car crashes, with the most recent crash occurring in February of this year.
He reports experiencing sharp, incapacitating pain, rated as a 12 out of 10, which prevents him from working.
He has had previous therapy for a few months after a second crash and had surgery after a third crash.
He has been experiencing numbness in his left arm and right leg, as well as spasms in his body.
He has been prescribed fentanyl in the past but has not received a new prescription for several weeks.
He reports fatigue since the accident and has been experiencing headaches, dizziness, and visual disturbances.
He denies any bruising or swelling but has noticed some bruising on occasion.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of multiple car crashes, including one in February of this year, and has undergone therapy and surgery for previous crashes.
He has also been to a pain clinic.
Review of systems: The patient reports experiencing numbness, spasms, headaches, dizziness, and visual disturbances.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has moderate range of movement in his neck, with pain on palpation on both the bony process and muscle.
He is unable to move his neck from side to side or bend it forward and backward without experiencing pain.
No bruising, swelling, or laceration is noted.
Test Results: An x-ray was performed prior to the visit, which did not show a fracture.
The doctor is planning to order an MRI to further evaluate the condition.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a neck sprain, which is a condition that can cause pain, numbness, and stiffness in the neck and surrounding areas.
Problem list: Neck sprain; suspected fracture (status: suspected); previous car crashes; history of therapy and surgery; pain; numbness; spasms; headaches; dizziness; visual disturbances; fatigue.
Plan:
I prescribed robaxin 1500mg every 6-8 hours as needed for pain.
I also recommended applying ice to the affected area and doing exercises to maintain range of motion.
I will refer the patient to physical therapy and schedule an MRI to further evaluate the condition.
If the patient's symptoms do not improve with conservative treatment, I may consider referring them to pain medication and exploring local injections.
The patient will be given a note to excuse them from work until further notice.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me after the MRI results are available to discuss further treatment options.
